topics.meaning: Mathematics is the study of patterns, numbers, shapes and the logical relationships between them.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Mathematics is often described as the study of patterns. Almost everything in mathematics, from numbers to shapes, is connected by patterns and rules. Mathematics helps us understand the world, solve everyday problems, and is used in science, art, music, sports and technology. Learning to notice patterns, ask questions and reason logically is what doing mathematics really means. It is not only about calculation but about discovering how things are related and why.
topics.easyExplanation: Maths is all about spotting patterns and understanding how numbers and shapes connect. It is used everywhere, from building bridges to making music.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: At its heart, mathematics is the search for patterns and the rules behind them.

examples: The way petals are arranged on a flower follows a mathematical pattern.

topics.meaning: A number pattern (number sequence) is a list of numbers arranged according to a fixed rule.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Numbers can be arranged in sequences that follow a rule, called number patterns or number sequences. Some important sequences are the counting numbers (1, 2, 3, ...), the odd numbers (1, 3, 5, ...), the even numbers (2, 4, 6, ...), the multiples of a number, the square numbers (1, 4, 9, 16, ...) and the triangular numbers (1, 3, 6, 10, ...). By studying a sequence, we can find its rule and use the rule to write the next numbers. Recognising the rule is the key to continuing or understanding a pattern.

stepByStepExplanation.title: See the sequence

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the rule
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Look at how each number relates to the next to discover the rule.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Continue the pattern
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use the rule to write the next numbers in the sequence.

topics.meaning: Number sequences can be shown using pictures or dots, helping us see why a pattern works.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Many number sequences become easier to understand when we draw them as pictures using dots or shapes. For example, square numbers (1, 4, 9, 16) can be drawn as dots forming squares, and triangular numbers (1, 3, 6, 10) as dots forming triangles. Visualising a sequence this way helps us see why the pattern grows the way it does. It also helps us discover new relationships, such as how a square can be split into two triangles. Pictures turn abstract numbers into something we can actually see.
topics.easyExplanation: We can draw number patterns as dots. Square numbers make square shapes and triangular numbers make triangles, which helps us 'see' the pattern.

topics.meaning: Different number sequences are connected; combining or comparing them reveals interesting relationships.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Number sequences are not separate; they are often linked to one another. For example, adding consecutive odd numbers gives square numbers (1 = 1, 1+3 = 4, 1+3+5 = 9), and adding two consecutive triangular numbers gives a square number (1+3 = 4, 3+6 = 9). Discovering these relations helps us understand numbers more deeply and predict results without long calculations. Finding how one sequence connects to another is a powerful part of mathematical thinking.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Sequences are linked

topics.meaning: Patterns also appear in shapes, which can grow or repeat according to a rule.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Patterns are not limited to numbers; they also appear in shapes. A shape sequence is a set of shapes arranged by a rule, where each new shape follows from the previous ones. Examples include regular polygons (triangle, square, pentagon, hexagon, with more and more sides) and sequences of growing patterns made from dots or sticks. By studying a shape pattern, we can predict what the next shape will look like and count its parts, such as the number of sides or corners.
topics.easyExplanation: Shapes can form patterns too, like polygons with more and more sides (triangle, square, pentagon). Finding the rule lets us draw the next shape.

topics.topic: Relation between Shape and Number Sequences
topics.meaning: Shape patterns and number patterns are connected, as counting parts of shapes often gives number sequences.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Shape patterns and number patterns are deeply linked. When we count parts of a growing shape pattern, such as the number of dots, sides or small squares, we often get a familiar number sequence. For example, the number of dots in a triangular pattern gives the triangular numbers (1, 3, 6, 10), and counting dots in square patterns gives the square numbers (1, 4, 9, 16). This shows that shapes and numbers are two ways of looking at the same pattern, and each helps us understand the other.
topics.easyExplanation: Shapes and numbers are linked: counting dots in triangle patterns gives triangular numbers, and counting dots in square patterns gives square numbers.

topics.meaning: A point is an exact location or position, shown by a tiny dot, and it has no length, breadth or thickness.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A point is the most basic idea in geometry. It marks an exact position or location in space and is shown by a small dot. A point has no size at all, meaning it has no length, no breadth and no thickness. Points are named using capital letters such as A, B or P. Everything in geometry, like lines and shapes, is built up from points.

topics.meaning: A line segment is the straight path between two points, having two endpoints and a fixed length.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A line segment is a straight path joining two points. These two points are called its endpoints. A line segment has a definite, fixed length that can be measured, because it has a clear beginning and end. A line segment between points A and B is written as segment AB. It is the shortest straight path between the two points.

topics.meaning: A line is a straight path that extends endlessly in both directions and has no endpoints.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A line is a straight path that goes on forever in both directions, with no beginning and no end. Because it never stops, a line has no fixed length and cannot be measured. We show a line by drawing a straight line with arrowheads at both ends to mean it continues endlessly. A line through two points A and B can be written as line AB. Through a single point, many lines can pass, but through two points, only one straight line can be drawn.
topics.easyExplanation: A line is a straight path that never ends in either direction. It has arrows on both ends and cannot be measured because it goes on forever.

topics.meaning: A ray is a straight path that starts at one point and extends endlessly in one direction.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A ray is a part of a line that has one endpoint and goes on forever in one direction. The fixed starting point is called the initial point, and the ray stretches endlessly the other way, shown by an arrowhead. Because it has no end on one side, a ray cannot be measured. A ray starting at A and passing through B is written as ray AB, with A as the starting point.

topics.meaning: An angle is formed when two rays start from the same point, called the vertex.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An angle is formed when two rays share the same starting point. The common starting point is called the vertex of the angle, and the two rays are called the arms or sides of the angle. An angle tells us how much one arm is turned from the other. An angle with vertex B and arms BA and BC is written as angle ABC, with the vertex letter in the middle. Angles are all around us, in the corners of books, the hands of a clock and the opening of a pair of scissors.
topics.easyExplanation: An angle is made when two rays meet at a point. That point is the vertex, and the rays are the arms. It shows how much one arm has turned.

topics.meaning: Angles are compared by how much they are 'open': a wider opening means a larger angle.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We can compare two angles to see which is larger. The size of an angle depends only on how much the arms are opened apart, not on the length of the arms. A wider opening between the arms means a bigger angle. We can compare angles by placing one on top of the other so their vertices and one arm match, and then seeing which angle's second arm is more open. This helps us decide which angle is greater, smaller or whether they are equal.
topics.easyExplanation: To compare angles, look at how wide they open, not how long the arms are. The one that opens wider is the bigger angle.

topics.meaning: A rotating arm model shows how an angle changes as one arm turns about the vertex.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We can understand angles better by making a simple model with two arms (strips) joined at one point so that one arm can rotate. As we rotate one arm about the joined point (the vertex), the angle between the two arms increases. This shows that an angle is really a measure of turning or rotation. The more we rotate the arm, the larger the angle becomes, until a full turn brings the arm back to where it started.
topics.easyExplanation: If you pin two strips at one end and turn one, the angle between them grows. This shows an angle is really an amount of turning.

topics.meaning: Some angles are special: a right angle is a quarter turn, a straight angle is a half turn, and a complete angle is a full turn.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Certain angles have special names because they come from simple amounts of turning. A right angle is a quarter of a full turn and looks like a perfect corner, such as the corner of a book. A straight angle is half a full turn, making a straight line. A complete angle (or full angle) is one full turn that brings the arm back to its starting position. These special angles help us describe and measure other angles.

topics.meaning: Angles are measured in degrees using a protractor, where a full turn is 360 degrees.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To measure angles exactly, we use a unit called the degree, written with the symbol for degrees. A full turn (complete angle) is 360 degrees, a half turn (straight angle) is 180 degrees, and a quarter turn (right angle) is 90 degrees. We measure an angle using an instrument called a protractor. We place the centre of the protractor on the vertex, line up one arm with the zero line, and read the mark where the other arm crosses the scale. This gives the angle's measure in degrees.
topics.easyExplanation: Angles are measured in degrees with a protractor. A full turn is 360 degrees, a straight angle 180, and a right angle 90.

topics.meaning: We can draw an angle of a given measure using a protractor and a ruler.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To draw an angle of a particular size, we use a protractor and a ruler. First we draw a ray to be one arm of the angle and mark its starting point as the vertex. We place the centre of the protractor on the vertex and align the ray with the zero line. We then find the required degree mark on the protractor and put a dot there. Finally, we join the vertex to this dot with a ruler to draw the second arm. The angle between the two arms is the angle we wanted.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Find the required degree on the scale and make a small dot there.

topics.topic: Types of Angles and their Measures
topics.meaning: Angles are classified by their measure as acute, right, obtuse, straight, reflex or complete.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Angles are grouped into types based on how big they are. An acute angle is less than 90 degrees. A right angle is exactly 90 degrees. An obtuse angle is more than 90 degrees but less than 180 degrees. A straight angle is exactly 180 degrees. A reflex angle is more than 180 degrees but less than 360 degrees. A complete angle is exactly 360 degrees. Knowing these types helps us quickly describe any angle by its size.
topics.easyExplanation: Angles have types by size: acute (less than 90), right (90), obtuse (90 to 180), straight (180), reflex (180 to 360) and complete (360).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Acute angle

stepByStepExplanation.title: Right and obtuse

stepByStepExplanation.title: Straight angle

stepByStepExplanation.title: Reflex and complete
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A reflex angle is between 180 and 360 degrees; a complete angle is 360 degrees.

examples: A 45-degree angle is acute, while a 120-degree angle is obtuse.
A 250-degree angle is a reflex angle.

topics.meaning: Numbers can describe and compare situations, such as how many people are taller than each person standing in a line.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Numbers do more than count; they can tell us useful information about a situation. In the book, children stand in a line and each says a number telling how many of the others are taller than them. By reading these numbers, we can figure out the arrangement and check whether the numbers make sense. This shows that numbers can carry information and that we can reason logically using them. Looking carefully at what each number means helps us solve interesting puzzles.

topics.meaning: A supercell is a cell in a grid whose number is greater than all the numbers next to it.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In a grid filled with numbers, a supercell is a cell whose number is larger than the numbers in all the cells immediately next to it. To find supercells, we compare each number with its neighbours. This activity helps us practise comparing numbers and noticing the largest values in different parts of a grid. Filling grids so that they have a certain number of supercells is an interesting puzzle that builds our understanding of comparing big numbers.
topics.easyExplanation: A supercell is a box in a grid holding a number bigger than all its neighbours. Finding them is a game of comparing numbers.

topics.topic: Patterns of Numbers on the Number Line
topics.meaning: A number line is a straight line on which numbers are marked in order, helping us see and compare numbers.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A number line is a straight line with numbers marked on it at equal distances, increasing as we move to the right. It helps us picture numbers, see their order, and compare them, since a number to the right is always greater than one to its left. We can also use the number line to mark patterns, find numbers between others, and estimate positions. The number line is a simple but powerful tool for understanding numbers and their relationships.
topics.easyExplanation: A number line is a straight line with numbers marked in order. Numbers grow as you go right, so it helps you compare and place numbers easily.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Draw the line
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Mark numbers at equal distances along a straight line.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Numbers increase right

stepByStepExplanation.title: Compare positions
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A number further right is greater than one to its left.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find and estimate
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: We use the number line to spot patterns, find in-between numbers and estimate positions.

topics.meaning: The number of digits and the place of each digit decide how large a number is.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Every number is made of digits, and the position (place) of each digit decides its value. A number with more digits is generally larger than one with fewer digits. By rearranging the digits of a number, we can make the largest and smallest possible numbers: putting the biggest digits in the highest places makes the largest number, and the smallest digits in the highest places (avoiding a leading zero) makes the smallest. Understanding digits and place value helps us read, write and compare large numbers correctly.
topics.easyExplanation: Numbers are built from digits, and where a digit sits decides its value. Arranging digits cleverly makes the biggest or smallest number.

topics.meaning: A palindrome is a number that reads the same forwards and backwards.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A palindromic number, or palindrome, is a number that stays the same when its digits are reversed, like 121 or 4554. We can create palindromes through a fun process: take a number, reverse its digits, add the two numbers, and repeat the process with the result; for many numbers, this eventually gives a palindrome. Exploring palindromes helps us practise addition and notice patterns in numbers. Palindromes appear in numbers, words and even dates.

topics.meaning: Kaprekar's magic number is 6174, reached by a special process on any four-digit number with different digits.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Indian mathematician D. R. Kaprekar discovered a special number, 6174, called Kaprekar's constant. Take any four-digit number that has at least two different digits. Arrange its digits in descending order to make the largest number, then in ascending order to make the smallest, and subtract the smaller from the larger. Repeat the process with each new result. Surprisingly, within a few steps you always reach 6174, and then it keeps repeating. This shows the hidden patterns and magic inside ordinary numbers.
topics.easyExplanation: Kaprekar's magic number is 6174. Pick any 4-digit number, make the biggest and smallest from its digits, subtract, and repeat. You always reach 6174.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Subtract
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Subtract the smaller number from the larger number.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Repeat to reach 6174
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Repeat the process with each result; you always reach 6174 within a few steps.

topics.meaning: Numbers on clocks and calendars follow patterns that we can explore and play with.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Clocks and calendars are full of numbers arranged in regular patterns. On a clock, the numbers 1 to 12 repeat every 12 hours, and the hands move in fixed ways. On a calendar, dates are arranged in rows and columns, so numbers in the same column differ by 7 (the days in a week). We can find many number patterns in these arrangements, such as adding dates in a small square on the calendar. Exploring clock and calendar numbers connects mathematics to everyday timekeeping.

topics.meaning: Mental math means doing calculations quickly in the head using clever strategies instead of writing every step.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Mental math is the skill of calculating in our head, without paper, by using smart strategies. For example, to add numbers we can group them into easy tens and hundreds, and to multiply we can break numbers into convenient parts. Adding numbers that make round figures first, or adjusting a number to a nearby round number and correcting later, makes calculation faster. Practising mental math builds confidence and speed and helps us estimate and check answers in daily life.
topics.easyExplanation: Mental math is doing sums in your head using clever tricks, like rounding to tens or breaking numbers into easy parts.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Calculate in the head

topics.meaning: Many number puzzles and tricks are based on hidden patterns that can be discovered and explained.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Numbers hide many surprising patterns that we can discover by playing with them. For example, certain sequences of additions or multiplications produce neat, predictable results, and some number tricks always give the same answer because of an underlying pattern. By trying examples, noticing what repeats, and reasoning about why, we can explain these patterns. Playing with number patterns makes mathematics fun and strengthens our ability to observe and reason.

topics.meaning: Some number problems, like the Collatz problem, are simple to state but no one has yet proved them.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Mathematics still has mysteries that nobody has solved. One famous example is the Collatz problem, also called the 3n+1 problem. Take any whole number: if it is even, halve it; if it is odd, multiply by 3 and add 1. Repeat the process with each result. For every number people have tried, the process eventually reaches 1, but no one has ever proved that it always works for every number. This shows that even simple-looking maths can hold deep, unsolved questions, and that there is always more to discover.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Keep applying the rule to each new result.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Every tried number reaches 1, but no one has proved it always will.

topics.meaning: Estimation means finding an answer that is close to the exact value, useful when an exact answer is not needed.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Estimation is the skill of quickly finding an approximate answer that is close enough for our needs. We estimate by rounding numbers to convenient values, such as the nearest ten, hundred or thousand, and then calculating with these rounded numbers. Estimation is useful in daily life, for example to guess the total cost of shopping or the time a journey will take. It also helps us check whether an exact answer is reasonable. A good estimate is close to the real value without needing exact calculation.

For example, in games where players take turns adding numbers up to a target, the winner can control the game by always reaching certain key totals. Finding winning strategies teaches us logical thinking and planning ahead.

topics.meaning: Data is a collection of facts or numbers, and organising it neatly makes it easier to understand.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Data means a collection of information, often in the form of numbers or facts, gathered for a purpose. For example, the favourite fruits of students in a class is data. Raw data collected just as it comes is hard to read, so we organise it, often using a tally marks table that records how many times each item occurs. The number of times something occurs is called its frequency. Organising data into a table makes it clear and ready for drawing graphs. Good data handling starts with collecting and organising data carefully.

topics.meaning: A pictograph represents data using pictures or symbols, where each symbol stands for a fixed number.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A pictograph (or pictogram) is a way of showing data using pictures or symbols. Each symbol stands for a certain number of items, given by a key, for example one picture of an apple may stand for 5 apples. To read a pictograph, we count the symbols in each row and multiply by the value of one symbol. Pictographs make data easy and attractive to understand at a glance. They are useful for comparing quantities quickly, though they suit whole-number multiples of the symbol value best.

topics.meaning: A bar graph shows data using rectangular bars whose lengths represent the values.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A bar graph is a way of showing data using bars of equal width, where the length (or height) of each bar represents the value of the item it stands for. The bars are drawn on a graph with two lines (axes): one shows the items and the other shows the values, with a chosen scale. A longer bar means a larger value, so we can compare quantities easily just by looking at the bar lengths. Bar graphs are clear, neat and very common for presenting data.
topics.easyExplanation: A bar graph uses bars to show data. The taller or longer the bar, the bigger the value, so you can compare amounts at a glance.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use bars for data
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A bar graph shows data using bars of equal width.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The length or height of each bar represents the value of that item.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: One axis lists the items and the other shows the values using a scale.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Compare the bars
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Longer bars mean larger values, so bars are easy to compare.

examples: A bar graph of favourite sports has a taller bar for the most popular sport.

topics.meaning: To draw a bar graph, we choose a scale and draw bars of equal width with lengths matching the values.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Drawing a bar graph follows clear steps. First, we draw two perpendicular lines (axes); along one we write the items and along the other we mark values using a suitable scale chosen to fit the data. Then, for each item, we draw a bar of equal width, with its length matching the item's value according to the scale. We leave equal gaps between bars and label both axes and the graph. A carefully drawn and labelled bar graph clearly presents the data.
topics.easyExplanation: To draw a bar graph: draw two axes, pick a scale, then draw equal-width bars whose lengths match the values, leaving equal gaps and adding labels.

topics.meaning: When presenting data, the design should be clear and honest, so graphs are easy to read and not misleading.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: How we present data matters as much as the data itself. A good graph is clear, neat and attractive, with proper labels, a sensible scale and tidy bars or symbols. We should design data presentations so they are easy to understand at a glance. At the same time, we must be honest: using an unfair scale or uneven symbols can make data look misleading, suggesting differences that are not real. Good data presentation is both pleasing to look at and truthful, helping the reader understand the data correctly.
topics.easyExplanation: A good graph looks neat and clear, with proper labels and a fair scale. We must design it honestly so it does not trick the reader.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Make it clear
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A good graph is neat, with proper labels and a sensible scale.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Be honest

stepByStepExplanation.title: Help the reader
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Good presentation helps the reader understand the data correctly at a glance.

examples: Using equal-sized symbols in a pictograph keeps it fair and clear.

topics.meaning: A common multiple is shared by two numbers' multiples, and a common factor divides both numbers exactly.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A factor of a number divides it exactly with no remainder, and a multiple of a number is got by multiplying it by whole numbers. When two numbers share a factor, it is called a common factor; for example, 1, 2, 3 and 6 are common factors of 12 and 18. When two numbers share a multiple, it is a common multiple; for example, 12 and 24 are common multiples of 4 and 6. The largest common factor and the smallest common multiple are especially useful for comparing and combining numbers.
topics.easyExplanation: A common factor divides two numbers exactly (like 6 divides 12 and 18). A common multiple is in both numbers' times-tables (like 12 for 4 and 6).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Recall factors and multiples
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A factor divides a number exactly; a multiple is got by multiplying a number by whole numbers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find common factors
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Numbers that divide both given numbers exactly are common factors.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find common multiples
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Numbers that appear in both numbers' multiples are common multiples.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Pick the special ones
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The greatest common factor and least common multiple are the most useful.

examples: Common factors of 12 and 18 are 1, 2, 3 and 6.
Common multiples of 4 and 6 include 12, 24 and 36.

importantKeywords.definition: A number that divides another exactly.

importantKeywords.definition: A number got by multiplying a number by whole numbers.

topics.meaning: A prime number has exactly two factors, 1 and itself, while a composite number has more than two factors.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Numbers can be sorted by how many factors they have. A prime number is a number greater than 1 that has exactly two factors: 1 and the number itself, such as 2, 3, 5, 7 and 11. A composite number has more than two factors, such as 4, 6, 8 and 9. The number 1 is special: it has only one factor, so it is neither prime nor composite. The number 2 is the only even prime number. Prime numbers are the building blocks of all other numbers.
topics.easyExplanation: A prime number has only two factors: 1 and itself (like 2, 3, 5, 7). A composite number has more factors (like 4, 6, 8). The number 1 is neither.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Count the factors
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Sort numbers by how many factors they have.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A prime number greater than 1 has exactly two factors: 1 and itself.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify composites

examples: 7 is prime because its only factors are 1 and 7.

topics.meaning: Two numbers are co-prime if their only common factor is 1.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Two numbers are called co-prime (or relatively prime) if the only factor they share is 1, meaning they have no other common factor. For example, 8 and 9 are co-prime because their only common factor is 1, even though neither of them is a prime number. Co-prime numbers need not be prime themselves. Recognising co-prime numbers is useful in many areas of mathematics, such as simplifying fractions and, as the book describes, in clever ways of safekeeping treasures using numbers.
topics.easyExplanation: Two numbers are co-prime if the only number that divides both is 1, like 8 and 9. They do not have to be prime themselves.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Check for only 1

stepByStepExplanation.title: Note they need not be prime
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Co-prime numbers like 8 and 9 need not be prime themselves.

stepByStepExplanation.title: See the use
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Co-prime numbers help in simplifying fractions and other clever number uses.

examples: 8 and 9 are co-prime because their only common factor is 1.

importantKeywords.definition: A factor shared by two numbers.

topics.meaning: Prime factorisation is writing a number as a product of its prime factors.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Every composite number can be written as a product of prime numbers, and this is called its prime factorisation. To find it, we keep dividing the number by prime numbers until only primes are left, then write the number as the product of these primes. For example, 12 = 2 x 2 x 3. The prime factorisation of a number is unique, except for the order of the factors. Prime factorisation is very useful for finding common factors, common multiples and for understanding the structure of numbers.
topics.easyExplanation: Prime factorisation means breaking a number down into the primes that multiply to make it, like 12 = 2 x 2 x 3.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Start with the number
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Take the composite number you want to factorise, such as 12.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Divide by primes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Keep dividing by prime numbers (2, 3, 5, ...) until only primes remain.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Write the product
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Express the number as the product of these prime factors, like 12 = 2 x 2 x 3.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Note it is unique
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The prime factorisation of a number is the same except for the order of factors.

importantKeywords.definition: Writing a number as a product of primes.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: 18 = 2 x 3 x 3 is its prime factorisation.

importantKeywords.definition: A factor that is a prime number.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: 3 is a prime factor of 12.

topics.meaning: Divisibility tests are quick rules to check whether a number is divisible by another without actually dividing.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Divisibility tests are handy rules that tell us if a number can be divided exactly by another, without doing the full division. A number is divisible by 2 if its last digit is even; by 5 if it ends in 0 or 5; by 10 if it ends in 0; by 3 if the sum of its digits is divisible by 3; by 9 if the sum of its digits is divisible by 9; and by 4 if the number formed by its last two digits is divisible by 4. These tests save time and help in factorisation and checking calculations.
topics.easyExplanation: Divisibility tests are quick checks, like: a number is divisible by 2 if it ends in an even digit, or by 3 if its digit-sum is divisible by 3.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Divisible by 4
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: By 4 if the number formed by the last two digits is divisible by 4.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: These quick rules save time in factorisation and checking answers.

topics.meaning: Prime numbers and factors lead to many interesting patterns and puzzles to explore.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Once we understand primes, factors and multiples, we can explore many fascinating number patterns and puzzles. For example, we can look for prime numbers using the Sieve of Eratosthenes, study pairs of primes that differ by 2 (called twin primes, like 11 and 13), and investigate interesting facts about how primes are spread among numbers. Playing with these ideas shows that numbers are full of surprises and deepens our understanding of how numbers are built from primes.

The Sieve of Eratosthenes finds primes by crossing out multiples.

topics.meaning: Perimeter is the total length of the boundary of a closed flat shape.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The perimeter of a closed flat shape is the total distance around its boundary. We find it by adding the lengths of all its sides. For a rectangle, the perimeter is 2 times (length plus breadth), and for a square it is 4 times the length of one side, since all sides are equal. Perimeter is measured in units of length such as centimetres or metres. Knowing perimeter is useful in real life, for example to find how much fencing is needed around a field or how much lace is needed to border a cloth.
topics.easyExplanation: Perimeter is the total length around the edge of a shape. Add up all the sides. For a square it is 4 times one side; for a rectangle, 2 x (length + breadth).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand perimeter
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Perimeter is the total length of the boundary of a closed shape.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Add the sides
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Find the perimeter by adding the lengths of all the sides.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use the rectangle rule
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Perimeter of a rectangle = 2 x (length + breadth).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use the square rule
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Perimeter of a square = 4 x side, since all four sides are equal.

examples: A rectangle 5 cm by 3 cm has perimeter 2 x (5 + 3) = 16 cm.
A square of side 4 cm has perimeter 4 x 4 = 16 cm.

topics.meaning: Area is the amount of flat surface a shape covers, measured in square units.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The area of a closed flat shape is the amount of surface it covers. Area is measured in square units, such as square centimetres or square metres. For a rectangle, the area is length multiplied by breadth, and for a square it is side multiplied by side. We can also find the area of a shape drawn on squared paper by counting the unit squares it covers, counting full squares as one and pairing up half squares. Area is useful for finding how much carpet covers a floor or how much paint covers a wall.

topics.meaning: The area of a triangle is related to the area of a rectangle, and equals half of base times height.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We can find the area of a triangle by relating it to a rectangle. A right-angled triangle is exactly half of a rectangle, so its area is half of length times breadth. More generally, the area of any triangle is half of its base multiplied by its height (the perpendicular distance from the base to the opposite corner). On squared paper, we can also estimate a triangle's area by counting the unit squares it covers. Understanding the area of a triangle helps us find areas of many shapes that can be split into triangles.

topics.meaning: A fraction represents equal shares of a whole, where the whole is divided into equal parts.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A fraction is a way of showing equal parts of a whole. When something is divided into equal shares, each share is a fraction of the whole. The number of equal parts the whole is divided into is shown by the bottom number (denominator), and the number of those parts we take is shown by the top number (numerator). For example, if a chapati is shared equally among 4 children, each gets one-fourth, written 1/4. Sharing equally is the basic idea behind fractions.

topics.topic: Fractional Units as Parts of a Whole
topics.meaning: A unit fraction has 1 as its numerator and represents one equal part of a whole.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A fractional unit, or unit fraction, is a fraction with 1 on top, such as 1/2, 1/3 or 1/4. It represents exactly one of the equal parts into which a whole is divided. The more parts the whole is divided into, the smaller each part becomes, so 1/8 is smaller than 1/4. Any fraction can be thought of as a number of these fractional units; for example, 3/4 means three units of 1/4. Understanding fractional units helps us understand all other fractions.
topics.easyExplanation: A unit fraction is one with 1 on top, like 1/4. It is one equal piece of a whole. The more pieces, the smaller each one, so 1/8 is smaller than 1/4.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A unit fraction has 1 as its numerator, like 1/2 or 1/5.

stepByStepExplanation.title: It is one part

stepByStepExplanation.title: More parts means smaller
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Dividing into more parts makes each part smaller, so 1/8 is less than 1/4.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Build other fractions
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Any fraction is a number of unit fractions, like 3/4 = three 1/4 units.

topics.meaning: Fractions let us measure quantities that are between whole numbers, using fractional units.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Not everything can be measured in whole numbers; often a length or quantity falls between two whole numbers. Fractions let us measure these in-between amounts using fractional units. For example, a length might be 2 and a half units, written 2 1/2. By dividing each unit into equal fractional parts, we can measure more precisely. This is why fractions are essential in measuring lengths, weights and amounts in everyday life, where exact whole numbers are rare.
topics.easyExplanation: Fractions help us measure amounts that fall between whole numbers, like 2 and a half metres, by splitting each unit into equal fractional parts.

stepByStepExplanation.title: See the need
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Many quantities fall between two whole numbers and cannot be measured in wholes alone.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use fractional units
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Divide each unit into equal fractional parts to measure in-between amounts.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Measure precisely
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: For example, a length can be measured as 2 1/2 units.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Apply to daily life
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Fractions are essential for measuring lengths, weights and amounts accurately.

examples: A ribbon may be 3 and a half units long, written 3 1/2.
Half a litre of milk is measured using the fraction 1/2.

importantKeywords.definition: An equal part of a unit used for measuring.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Half a metre uses the fractional unit 1/2.

importantKeywords.definition: To find the size or amount of something.

importantKeywords.definition: A quantity falling between two whole numbers.

topics.topic: Marking Fraction Lengths on the Number Line
topics.meaning: Fractions can be shown as points on the number line by dividing each unit into equal parts.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Fractions are numbers, so they have their own places on the number line. To mark a fraction, we divide the gap between two whole numbers into as many equal parts as the denominator shows, then count the parts given by the numerator. For example, to mark 3/4, we divide the gap between 0 and 1 into 4 equal parts and count 3 parts from 0. This shows clearly that fractions lie between whole numbers and helps us compare and order them by their positions.
topics.easyExplanation: To put a fraction on the number line, split each unit into equal parts (the denominator) and count up (the numerator). So 3/4 is 3 of 4 parts between 0 and 1.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Fractions are numbers
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Every fraction has its own place on the number line.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Divide the unit
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Split the gap between two whole numbers into equal parts equal to the denominator.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Count the parts
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Count the number of parts shown by the numerator from the left whole number.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Mark the point
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: For 3/4, divide 0 to 1 into 4 parts and count 3 to mark the fraction.

examples: To mark 1/2, divide the space between 0 and 1 into two equal parts and take one.
To mark 3/4, divide 0 to 1 into 4 equal parts and count 3 from 0.

importantKeywords.definition: A straight line with numbers marked in order.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Fractions are placed on the number line.

importantKeywords.definition: The bottom number showing equal parts.

topics.meaning: A mixed fraction has a whole number part and a fractional part, and equals an improper fraction.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Fractions can be of different kinds. A proper fraction has a numerator smaller than its denominator, like 3/4, and is less than 1. An improper fraction has a numerator equal to or greater than its denominator, like 7/4, and is 1 or more. A mixed fraction (mixed number) combines a whole number and a proper fraction, like 1 3/4. Every improper fraction can be written as a mixed fraction and the other way round, for example 7/4 = 1 3/4. This helps us read and use fractions greater than 1 more easily.
topics.easyExplanation: A mixed fraction is a whole number plus a fraction, like 1 3/4. It is the same as an improper fraction like 7/4.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Proper fraction
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A fraction less than 1, with numerator smaller than denominator, like 3/4.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Improper fraction
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A fraction of 1 or more, with numerator at least the denominator, like 7/4.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Mixed fraction
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A whole number plus a proper fraction, like 1 3/4.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Convert between them
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Every improper fraction equals a mixed fraction, for example 7/4 = 1 3/4.

examples: 7/4 written as a mixed fraction is 1 3/4.
2 1/2 written as an improper fraction is 5/2.

importantKeywords.definition: A fraction less than 1.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: 3/5 is a proper fraction.

importantKeywords.definition: A fraction equal to or greater than 1.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: 9/4 is an improper fraction.

importantKeywords.definition: A whole number with a proper fraction.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: 1 1/2 is a mixed fraction.

revisionNotes: Proper fraction < 1 (e.g. 3/4).

topics.meaning: Equivalent fractions are different fractions that represent the same value.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Equivalent fractions are fractions that look different but stand for the same amount, like 1/2, 2/4 and 4/8. We get an equivalent fraction by multiplying or dividing both the numerator and the denominator by the same number (not zero). For example, multiplying 1/2 by 3/3 gives 3/6. Dividing both parts by their common factor gives a simpler equivalent fraction; when no common factor (other than 1) is left, the fraction is in its simplest form. Equivalent fractions help us compare, add and subtract fractions.
topics.easyExplanation: Equivalent fractions are different-looking fractions worth the same, like 1/2 = 2/4. Multiply or divide top and bottom by the same number to make them.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Same value, different form
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Equivalent fractions represent the same amount, like 1/2 and 2/4.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Multiply both parts
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Multiply numerator and denominator by the same number to get an equivalent fraction.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Divide both parts
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Divide numerator and denominator by a common factor to simplify.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Reach simplest form
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: When only 1 is the common factor, the fraction is in its simplest form.

examples: 1/2, 2/4 and 3/6 are equivalent fractions.
6/8 simplifies to 3/4 by dividing both parts by 2.

importantKeywords.definition: Different fractions with the same value.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: 2/4 and 1/2 are equivalent.

importantKeywords.definition: A fraction with no common factor (except 1) in numerator and denominator.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: 3/4 is in simplest form.

importantKeywords.definition: A number dividing both numerator and denominator.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: 2 is a common factor of 6 and 8.

topics.quickSummary: Equivalent fractions are different fractions with the same value, made by multiplying or dividing both parts by the same number; dividing to remove common factors gives the simplest form.
revisionNotes: Equivalent fractions have the same value (1/2 = 2/4).

topics.meaning: Fractions are compared by making their denominators the same or by using equivalent fractions.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To compare two fractions and find which is greater, it is easiest when they have the same denominator: then the fraction with the larger numerator is greater. If the denominators are different, we change the fractions into equivalent fractions with a common denominator and then compare the numerators. For fractions with the same numerator, the one with the smaller denominator is greater, because fewer parts means bigger parts. Comparing fractions lets us order them from smallest to largest.
topics.easyExplanation: To compare fractions, make their bottoms the same, then the bigger top wins. If the tops are equal, the smaller bottom is the bigger fraction.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Same denominator is easy
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: When denominators are equal, the fraction with the larger numerator is greater.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Make a common denominator
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If denominators differ, change to equivalent fractions with the same denominator.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Then compare numerators
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: With a common denominator, compare the numerators to decide which is greater.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Same numerator rule
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If numerators are equal, the smaller denominator gives the greater fraction.

examples: 3/5 is greater than 2/5 because they share a denominator and 3 > 2.
1/3 is greater than 1/4 because, with equal numerators, the smaller denominator wins.

importantKeywords.definition: The same denominator made for two fractions.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: For 1/2 and 1/3, a common denominator is 6.

importantKeywords.definition: To decide which value is greater.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: We compare 2/3 and 3/4 by using a common denominator.

topics.meaning: Fractions are added or subtracted by making their denominators the same, then adding or subtracting the numerators.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To add or subtract fractions, they must have the same denominator. If the denominators are already the same, we simply add or subtract the numerators and keep the denominator unchanged. If the denominators are different, we first change the fractions into equivalent fractions with a common denominator, then add or subtract the numerators. For mixed fractions, we can add the whole parts and the fractional parts separately, or first convert to improper fractions. The final answer is usually written in its simplest form.
topics.easyExplanation: To add or subtract fractions, make the bottoms the same, then add or subtract the tops. Keep the bottom the same and simplify the answer.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Need same denominator
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Fractions can be added or subtracted only when their denominators are the same.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Make denominators equal

stepByStepExplanation.title: Add or subtract numerators
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Add or subtract the numerators and keep the common denominator.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Simplify the answer
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Write the result in its simplest form, converting to a mixed fraction if needed.

topics.meaning: Fractions have a long history, and ancient civilisations, including in India, developed ways to write and use them.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Fractions are a very old idea. Ancient civilisations such as the Egyptians, Babylonians and Indians used fractions thousands of years ago for trade, measuring land and sharing goods. Indian mathematicians made important contributions to how fractions are written and worked with, including ways of writing mixed numbers. Learning about the history of fractions shows that mathematics has grown over a long time through the work of many cultures, and that ideas we use today were built up gradually by people across the world.
topics.easyExplanation: Fractions are very old. Egyptians, Babylonians and Indians all used them long ago for trade and measuring, and Indian mathematicians helped shape how we write them.

topics.meaning: Many beautiful designs can be drawn precisely using only a compass and a ruler.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Geometric constructions let us draw accurate figures using simple tools: a ruler (straightedge) for straight lines and a compass for circles and arcs. By combining circles and lines, we can create attractive artwork and patterns, such as flower-like designs made from overlapping circles of the same radius. These activities show that careful use of a compass and ruler can produce precise and beautiful shapes. Learning to construct figures builds accuracy and prepares us for more advanced geometry.

topics.meaning: Squares and rectangles have special properties of equal sides and right angles that define them.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A rectangle is a four-sided figure in which all four angles are right angles (90 degrees) and opposite sides are equal in length. A square is a special rectangle in which all four sides are also equal. Both shapes have their opposite sides parallel. Knowing these properties, the right angles and the equal sides, is essential before we can construct them accurately. Understanding what makes a square and a rectangle special is the first step to drawing them.
topics.easyExplanation: A rectangle has four right angles and equal opposite sides. A square is a rectangle with all four sides equal too.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Define a rectangle
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A rectangle has four right angles and opposite sides equal.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Define a square
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A square is a rectangle with all four sides equal.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Note parallel sides
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Both shapes have their opposite sides parallel.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use the properties
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: These properties of right angles and equal sides guide how we construct the shapes.

examples: A book cover is shaped like a rectangle with right-angled corners.
A carrom board is shaped like a square with all four sides equal.

topics.meaning: Squares and rectangles can be constructed accurately using a ruler, compass and right angles.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To construct a rectangle of given side lengths, we draw one side with a ruler, then make right angles at its ends (using a protractor or compass), draw the other sides of the correct lengths, and join them. For a square, all four sides are equal, so we use the same length for each side. The key is to make accurate right angles and measure side lengths carefully. Following the steps in order gives a precise square or rectangle.
topics.easyExplanation: To construct a rectangle: draw one side, make right angles at its ends, draw the other sides to the right length, and join. For a square, all sides are equal.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Draw the other sides
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Draw the remaining sides to the correct lengths along the right angles.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Join and check
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Join the sides to complete the figure; for a square, make all sides equal.

topics.meaning: Exploring rectangles reveals relationships, such as how different rectangles can have the same perimeter or area.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: By exploring rectangles, we discover interesting relationships. Different rectangles can have the same perimeter but different areas, and rectangles with the same area can have different perimeters. For example, rectangles measuring 1 by 6 and 2 by 3 both have an area of 6 square units but different perimeters. Among all rectangles with a fixed perimeter, the one closest to a square has the largest area. Exploring such relationships deepens our understanding of perimeter, area and the special nature of the square.

topics.topic: Exploring Diagonals of Rectangles and Squares
topics.meaning: The diagonals of rectangles and squares have special properties, such as being equal in length.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A diagonal is a straight line joining two opposite corners of a shape. A rectangle has two diagonals, and they are always equal in length and cut each other into two equal parts at their crossing point (they bisect each other). In a square, the two diagonals are not only equal and bisect each other, but they also cross at right angles. Exploring diagonals by drawing and measuring them helps us discover and confirm these special properties of squares and rectangles.
topics.easyExplanation: A diagonal joins opposite corners. In a rectangle, the two diagonals are equal and cross at their middles. In a square, they also cross at right angles.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: In a square, the diagonals are also equal and cross at right angles.

topics.topic: Points Equidistant from Two Given Points
topics.meaning: The points that are equally far from two given points lie on the perpendicular bisector of the segment joining them.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: If we look for all the points that are the same distance from two given points, we find that they form a straight line. This line passes through the midpoint of the segment joining the two points and is perpendicular to it; it is called the perpendicular bisector. Every point on this line is equidistant (equally distant) from the two given points. We can construct this line using a compass, by drawing equal arcs from both points and joining their crossing points. This idea is useful in many geometric constructions.
topics.easyExplanation: All points equally far from two points lie on one line: the perpendicular bisector. It cuts the joining segment in half at a right angle.

topics.meaning: A line of symmetry divides a figure into two identical halves that are mirror images of each other.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A figure has line symmetry (reflection symmetry) if a line can be drawn through it so that one half is the exact mirror image of the other. This line is called the line of symmetry or axis of symmetry. If we fold the figure along this line, the two halves match exactly. A shape can have no line of symmetry, one, or many: a rectangle has two lines of symmetry, a square has four, and a circle has countless lines of symmetry. Many shapes in nature and design, like leaves and the letter A, have line symmetry.
topics.easyExplanation: A line of symmetry splits a shape into two matching mirror halves. Fold along it and the halves overlap exactly. A square has four such lines.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Define line symmetry
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A figure has line symmetry if a line divides it into two mirror-image halves.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Name the line
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: This dividing line is called the line of symmetry or axis of symmetry.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Test by folding

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Shapes vary: a rectangle has 2 lines, a square has 4, and a circle has countless.

importantKeywords.definition: Balanced, matching arrangement of parts.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A square shows symmetry.

topics.quickSummary: A line of symmetry divides a figure into two identical mirror-image halves that match when folded; shapes may have none, one or many lines (square has 4, circle countless).
revisionNotes: Line of symmetry splits a shape into mirror halves.
Folding along it makes halves match.
Also called the axis of symmetry.
Rectangle: 2 lines; square: 4; circle: countless.

topics.meaning: A figure has rotational symmetry if it looks the same after being turned by less than a full turn about its centre.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A figure has rotational symmetry if, when it is rotated about a fixed central point by some angle less than a full turn, it looks exactly the same as before. The fixed point is the centre of rotation. The number of times the figure matches its original position during one full turn is called the order of rotational symmetry. For example, a square has rotational symmetry of order 4, because it looks the same after every quarter turn, and a equilateral triangle has order 3. Every figure looks the same after a full turn, but only special figures match before completing the full turn.
topics.easyExplanation: A shape has rotational symmetry if it looks the same when turned part of the way around its centre. A square matches 4 times in a full turn (order 4).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Rotate about a centre
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Turn the figure about a fixed central point called the centre of rotation.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Check if it matches
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If it looks the same after a turn less than full, it has rotational symmetry.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Count the matches
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The number of matches in one full turn is the order of rotational symmetry.

stepByStepExplanation.title: See examples
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A square has order 4 (quarter turns) and an equilateral triangle has order 3.

examples: A square looks the same after every 90-degree turn, so its order of rotational symmetry is 4.
A windmill or fan with equal blades has rotational symmetry.

importantKeywords.definition: Looking the same after a partial turn about a centre.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A square has rotational symmetry.

importantKeywords.definition: The fixed point a figure is turned about.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The centre of a square is its centre of rotation.

importantKeywords.definition: The number of matches in one full turn.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A square has order 4.

topics.quickSummary: A figure has rotational symmetry if it looks the same after turning less than a full turn about its centre; the number of matches in a full turn is its order (square = 4, triangle = 3).
revisionNotes: Rotational symmetry = looks same after a partial turn.
Turned about the centre of rotation.
Order = matches in one full turn.
Square: order 4; equilateral triangle: order 3.

topics.topic: Bela's Building of Fun (Above and Below Zero)
topics.meaning: Numbers can go below zero; floors above the ground and below it (basements) show positive and negative numbers.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter begins with a building that has floors above the ground and floors below the ground (basements). The ground floor is taken as zero. Floors above zero are counted as positive numbers (1, 2, 3, ...) and floors below zero, the basements, are counted as negative numbers (-1, -2, -3, ...). This everyday picture shows that numbers can go below zero. Moving up means adding (going towards positive) and moving down means subtracting (going towards negative), which introduces the idea of negative numbers and integers.
topics.easyExplanation: Think of a building where the ground floor is zero. Floors above are positive numbers and basements below are negative numbers. This shows numbers can go below zero.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Set zero at the ground
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The ground floor of the building is taken as zero.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Floors above are positive
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Floors above the ground are positive numbers: 1, 2, 3, ...

stepByStepExplanation.title: Basements are negative
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Floors below the ground (basements) are negative numbers: -1, -2, -3, ...

stepByStepExplanation.title: Up and down
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Going up adds (towards positive) and going down subtracts (towards negative).

examples: The second basement of a building can be labelled -2.
Going from floor 2 down to basement 1 (-1) is like subtracting.

importantKeywords.definition: A number less than zero, written with a minus sign.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: -3 is a negative number.

importantKeywords.definition: A number greater than zero.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: 5 is a positive number.

importantKeywords.definition: The number that separates positive and negative numbers.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Zero is neither positive nor negative.

topics.quickSummary: Using a building with floors above and basements below the ground (zero), we see numbers can be positive (above) or negative (below), introducing negative numbers and integers.
revisionNotes: Ground floor = zero.
Floors above = positive numbers.
Basements below = negative numbers.
Up adds; down subtracts.

topics.meaning: Positive and negative tokens model integers, where one positive and one negative token cancel out to zero.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The token model is a simple way to understand integers and their addition. We use two kinds of tokens: positive tokens (standing for +1) and negative tokens (standing for -1). A pair of one positive and one negative token cancels out to make zero, called a zero pair. To find the value of a collection of tokens, we cancel out all zero pairs and count what is left. This model makes it easy to see how adding positive and negative numbers works, for example how 3 + (-2) leaves 1.
topics.easyExplanation: Use positive and negative tokens for numbers. One positive and one negative token cancel to zero. Cancel pairs and count what is left to add integers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use two kinds of tokens
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Positive tokens stand for +1 and negative tokens stand for -1.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Make zero pairs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: One positive and one negative token together make zero, a zero pair.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Cancel the pairs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Remove all zero pairs from a collection of tokens.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Count what remains
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The leftover tokens give the value, showing how integer addition works.

examples: 3 positive and 2 negative tokens cancel to leave 1, so 3 + (-2) = 1.
4 negative and 4 positive tokens cancel completely to make zero.

importantKeywords.definition: Using positive and negative tokens to represent integers.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The token model shows 2 + (-2) = 0.

importantKeywords.definition: One positive and one negative token that cancel to zero.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A +1 and a -1 token make a zero pair.

importantKeywords.definition: A whole number that can be positive, negative or zero.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: -2 is an integer.

topics.quickSummary: The token model represents integers with positive and negative tokens; a positive and a negative token form a zero pair, and cancelling pairs and counting the rest shows integer addition.
revisionNotes: Positive tokens = +1, negative tokens = -1.
A positive and a negative token = zero pair.
Cancel zero pairs.
Count remaining tokens for the value.

topics.meaning: Integers, including negative numbers, are used in real life for temperature, money, depth and direction.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Negative numbers and integers appear in many real-life situations, not just in textbooks. Temperatures below zero degrees are written as negative numbers, like -5 degrees Celsius on a cold day. In money, what we owe (debt) can be shown as a negative amount, while savings are positive. Depths below sea level are negative while heights above sea level are positive. Even directions, like moving left or right, can be shown with positive and negative numbers. Recognising integers around us shows why negative numbers are useful and important.
topics.easyExplanation: Negative numbers are everywhere: freezing temperatures (-5 degrees), money owed, depths below sea level, and directions all use integers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Temperature
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Temperatures below zero are written as negative numbers, like -5 degrees Celsius.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Money
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Money owed (debt) is shown as negative; savings are positive.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Depth and height
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Depths below sea level are negative; heights above sea level are positive.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Direction
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Opposite directions, like left and right, can be shown using negative and positive numbers.

examples: A temperature of 4 degrees below zero is written as -4 degrees Celsius.
A place 200 metres below sea level can be shown as -200 metres.

importantKeywords.definition: A whole number that is positive, negative or zero.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: -7 is an integer.

importantKeywords.definition: A depth lower than the sea surface, shown as negative.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: -100 m means 100 m below sea level.

importantKeywords.definition: Money that is owed, shown as a negative amount.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Owing 50 rupees can be written as -50.

topics.quickSummary: Integers, including negative numbers, are used in real life for temperatures below zero, money owed, depths below sea level and directions, showing why they are useful.
revisionNotes: Negative numbers appear in real life.
Temperature below zero = negative.
Debt negative, savings positive.
Below sea level negative; directions use integers.

topics.meaning: Integers can be added and subtracted using the number line or token model, following clear rules.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We can add and subtract integers using the number line or the token model. On the number line, adding a positive number means moving to the right and adding a negative number means moving to the left; subtracting reverses the direction. Using tokens, we combine tokens and cancel zero pairs. From these, we see rules such as: adding two negatives gives a negative, and adding a positive and a negative gives the difference with the sign of the larger. Exploring these helps us calculate confidently with positive and negative numbers.
topics.easyExplanation: Add and subtract integers using a number line (right for positive, left for negative) or tokens. Two negatives add to a negative; mixed signs give the difference.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use the number line
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Adding a positive moves right; adding a negative moves left on the number line.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Subtract by reversing
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Subtracting an integer reverses the direction of movement.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use tokens
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Combine positive and negative tokens and cancel zero pairs to find the result.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Notice the rules
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Two negatives add to a negative; a positive and a negative give the difference with the larger one's sign.

examples: On a number line, 3 + (-5) moves left to give -2.
(-4) + (-3) = -7, since two negatives add to a negative.

importantKeywords.definition: A line with numbers in order used to add/subtract.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: We add integers by moving along the number line.

importantKeywords.definition: Combining integers to get a sum.
Whether a number is positive (+) or negative (-).
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The sign of -6 is negative.

topics.quickSummary: Integers are added and subtracted using the number line (right for positive, left for negative) or the token model; rules emerge, like two negatives giving a negative.
revisionNotes: Number line: right for +, left for -.
Subtracting reverses the direction.
Tokens: combine and cancel zero pairs.
Two negatives add to a negative.

topics.topic: A Pinch of History (of Zero and Negative Numbers)
topics.meaning: Zero and negative numbers have a rich history, with India playing a key role in developing the idea of zero.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The ideas of zero and negative numbers took a long time to develop in history. Ancient Indian mathematicians, especially Brahmagupta, made major contributions by treating zero as a number and giving rules for working with positive and negative numbers (which he linked to fortunes and debts). The concept of zero, developed in India, later spread to the rest of the world and became essential to all of mathematics. Learning this history shows the great contribution of Indian mathematics and how important ideas grow over time.
topics.easyExplanation: Zero and negative numbers have a long history. Indian mathematicians like Brahmagupta first treated zero as a number and gave rules for negatives, ideas that spread worldwide.
